communicative_purpose: To express that someone is missed when they are not present
trigger_situations: Missing a friend, partner, or family member; expressing desire for someone's presence
contexts: Texting, phone calls, letters, social media, separation situations
variations: tu me manques beaucoup, tu nous manques, tu lui manques
common_mistakes: Confusing with 'tu me manques' meaning literally 'you are missing to me'; literal translation of English 'miss'
collocates: beaucoup, toujours, tout le temps, bien
example_1: Tu me manques tellement ! J'ai hâte de te revoir.
example_2: Elle me manque beaucoup depuis qu'elle est partie.
example_1_translation: I miss you so much ! I can't wait to see you again.
example_2_translation: I miss her a lot since she left.
example_3_translation: We miss you, come back soon!

communicative_purpose: To express strong positive reaction to something
trigger_situations: Reacting to good news, exciting plans, fun activities
contexts: Casual conversations, texting, social media reactions, celebrations
variations: c'est trop bien, c'est super cool, c'est génial, c'est naze (sarcastic)
common_mistakes: Using it sarcastically without context; overusing in formal settings
example_1: C'est trop bien ce concert ! J'adore ce groupe.
example_2: Elle a réussi son exam, c'est trop bien !
example_3: Trop bien cette idée, on y va !
example_1_translation: This concert is so good! I love this group.
example_2_translation: She passed her exam, that’s so good!
example_3_translation: This idea is great, here we go!

communicative_purpose: To enthusiastically agree to join an activity or accept a proposal
trigger_situations: Accepting invitations, agreeing to plans, confirming participation
contexts: Making plans with friends, party invitations, group activities
variations: je suis partante, je suis partant, je suis chaud/chauffe (slang), ça me dit
common_mistakes: Gender agreement with 'partant/partante'; confusing with 'je pars' (I'm leaving)
example_1: Pour la soirée de vendredi ? Je suis partante !
example_2: Tu veux aller au cinéma ? Je suis partant aussi !
example_3: Je suis partante pour le resto, tu choisis où ?
example_1_translation: For Friday evening? I'm up for it!
example_2_translation: Do you want to go to the cinema? I'm up for it too!
example_3_translation: I'm up for the restaurant, where do you choose?

communicative_purpose: To emphatically agree or confirm something
trigger_situations: Strong agreement, confirming plans, showing enthusiasm
contexts: Youth slang, friends chatting, texting, casual hangouts
variations: grave !, ouais grave !, carrément, bigre (older slang)
common_mistakes: Using it sarcastically without clear context; sounding too casual in formal situations
example_1: T'as raison, grave ! On y va direct.
example_2: C'était super bon ce burger, grave !
example_3: Tu viens ? Grave ouais, j'y serai !
example_1_translation: You're right, serious! Let's go straight there.
example_2_translation: This burger was really good, seriously!
example_3_translation: Are you coming? Seriously yeah, I'll be there!

communicative_purpose: To warmly welcome a guest and invite them to relax freely
trigger_situations: Welcoming guests to your home, hosting friends, house parties
contexts: Home visits, dinner parties, sleepovers, welcoming visitors
variations: fais comme chez toi, installe-toi, sens-toi chez toi, fais comme chez nous
common_mistakes: Literal translation issues; using in non-host situations
collocates: Bienvenue, installe-toi, serveur, ouvrir la porte
example_1: Entre, entre ! Fais comme chez toi, y'a pas de souci.
example_2: Bienvenue ! Fais comme chez toi, je t'en prie.
example_3: Fais comme chez toi, le fridge est ouverte.
example_1_translation: Come in, come in! Make yourself at home, no problem.
example_2_translation: Welcome ! Make yourself at home, please.
example_3_translation: Make yourself at home, the fridge is open.

communicative_purpose: To ask who's calling, who's at the door, or who's being discussed
trigger_situations: Phone calls, doorbell, identifying people in photos, confusion about people
contexts: Texting, phone conversations, door intercoms, social situations
variations: c'est qui, c'est qui qui (emphatic), c'est qui ça, c'est qui l'autre
common_mistakes: Double 'qui' being considered non-standard but very common in spoken French
collocates: qui, qui c'est, là, à la porte, au téléphone
example_1: C'est qui qui frappe à la porte à cette heure ?
example_2: C'est qui dans la photo ? Je connais pas ce mec.
example_3: Allo ? C'est qui, j'ai pas ton numéro ?
example_1_translation: Who's knocking at the door at this hour?
example_2_translation: Who is that in the photo? I don't know this guy.
example_3_translation: Hello ? Who is it, I don’t have your number?

communicative_purpose: To casually ask someone what they're currently doing or to start a conversation
trigger_situations: Texting, casual meetups, checking in on friends, making plans
contexts: SMS, messaging apps, phone calls, informal encounters
variations: tu fais quoi, tu fous quoi (vulgar), tu branles quoi (vulgar), tu étais où
common_mistakes: Confusing with 'tu vas faire quoi' (what will you do); using formal register in casual contexts
collocates: maintenant, là, en ce moment, ici
example_1: Yo, tu fais quoi ce soir ? On sort ?
example_2: Tu fais quoi en ce moment ? Toujours au bureau ?
example_3: T'es où ? Tu fais quoi ? Réponds-moi !
example_1_translation: Yo, what are you doing tonight? Shall we go out?
example_2_translation: What are you doing at the moment? Still in the office?
example_3_translation: Where are you? What are you doing ? Answer me!

primary_function: Slang expression of pity or mockery
communicative_purpose: To express that something is disappointing, sad, or laughable
trigger_situations: Reacting to sad situations, mocking poor choices, feeling sympathy
contexts: Friend gossip, failed attempts, pity parties, comedic disappointment
variations: c'est sniffe, c'est pathétique, c'est triste, c'est la honte
common_mistakes: Spelling 'sniffe' vs 'snif' or 'sniffer'; being too harsh in serious situations
example_1: Il a encore raté son coup, c'est sniffe quand même.
example_2: Elle pleure depuis une heure, c'est sniffe à voir.
example_3: Ce film était vraiment sniffe, on s'ennuyait.
example_1_translation: He missed his shot again, it's still a shame.
example_2_translation: She's been crying for an hour, it's shocking to see.
example_3_translation: This film was really bad, we were bored.

meaning: I don't get it / I'm not getting it
communicative_purpose: To admit confusion or lack of understanding about something
trigger_situations: Not understanding explanations, confusing instructions, feeling lost
contexts: School, work explanations, complex topics, technology issues
variations: je capte rien, j'y capte que dalle (vulgar), je comprends rien, nada
common_mistakes: Using 'je capte' alone (means 'I receive signal'); overusing in formal settings
collocates: à ce cours, à cette explication, à ton truc, là
example_1: Désolé mec, je capte rien à ton histoire là.
example_2: Le prof parle trop vite, je capte rien au cours.
example_3: C'est du Chinois pour moi, je capte rien.
example_1_translation: Sorry dude, I don't understand your story there.
example_2_translation: The teacher speaks too quickly, I don't catch anything during class.
example_3_translation: It's Chinese to me, I don't understand anything.

communicative_purpose: To express disbelief, ask for clarification, or show interest in gossip
trigger_situations: Reacting to surprising news, gossip, incredible stories, teasing
contexts: Friend conversations, social media, texting, casual storytelling
variations: genre ?, genre !, sérieux ?, c'est pas vrai ?, sérieux pas
common_mistakes: Overusing it to the point of losing meaning; using it seriously when ironic
example_1: Il a réussi à séduire la fille la plus populaires du lycée ? Genre ?!
example_2: Tu sors avec Marc ? Genre ! Il est trop beau.
example_3: C'est la honte totale. Genre, j'ose plus rien dire.
example_1_translation: Did he manage to seduce the most popular girl in school? Gender ?!
example_2_translation: Are you going out with Marc? Gender ! He's too handsome.
example_3_translation: It's a total shame. Like, I don’t dare say anything anymore.

primary_function: Slang expression of tiredness or frustration
communicative_purpose: To express being fed up, very tired, or emotionally drained
trigger_situations: Long day, stress, being overwhelmed, dramatic exhaustion
contexts: After work/school, stressful situations, emotional exhaustion
variations: je suis over, je suis overée, je suis HS (hors service), j'en peux plus
common_mistakes: Gender agreement; confusing with English 'over' meaning finished
example_1: Je suis over après cette semaine de ouf, j'ai besoin de vacances.
example_2: Trop de drama avec elle, je suis overée.
example_3: HS aujourd'hui, on annule tout, désolée.
example_1_translation: I'm exhausted after this crazy week, I need a vacation.
example_2_translation: Too much drama with her, I'm overwhelmed.
example_3_translation: HS today, we're canceling everything, sorry.

meaning: it's dead / there's nothing going on
primary_function: Expression indicating emptiness or lack of options
communicative_purpose: To say there's no activity, no options, or nothing available
trigger_situations: Empty venue, no plans available, closed places, dead conversation
contexts: Nightlife, party scenes, making plans, group chats
variations: c'est mort, c'est vide, y'a rien à faire, c'est naze
common_mistakes: Confusing literal death meaning; using it literally in serious contexts
example_1: Le centre-ville c'est mort ce soir, tous les bars sont fermés.
example_2: Cette soirée est morte, personne parle à personne.
example_3: C'est mort comme plan, personne va venir.
example_1_translation: The city center is dead tonight, all the bars are closed.
example_2_translation: This party is dead, no one talks to anyone.
example_3_translation: It's a dead plan, no one is going to come.

primary_function: Slang expression of love or strong liking
communicative_purpose: To express enthusiasm, love, or strong approval in casual contexts
trigger_situations: Expressing love for music, food, people, activities, trends
contexts: Social media, texting, concerts, food pics, fan culture
variations: je kiffe, je kiff, je kiffe grave, c'est mon kiff
common_mistakes: Overuse leading to dilution; considering it too vulgar for some contexts
example_1: Cette chanson est incroyable, je kiffe trop !
example_2: Le sushi dans ce restaurant, je kiffe bin !
example_3: C'est mon kiff ultimate, je peux pas m'en passer.
example_1_translation: This song is incredible, I love it so much!
example_2_translation: I love the sushi in this restaurant!
example_3_translation: It's my ultimate favorite, I can't live without it.

meaning: did you see / have you seen
communicative_purpose: To draw attention to something or ask if someone witnessed an event
trigger_situations: Sharing news, gossip, viral content, important events
contexts: Texting, social media, conversations, group chats
variations: t'as vu, t'as vu ça, t'as vu qu'est-ce qui s'est passé, t'as vu le dernier épisode
common_mistakes: Confusing with 'tu as vu' meaning literally 'you saw'; register mismatch
collocates: ça, la news, la story, le dernier post
example_1: T'as vu le dernier post de Julie ? Elle a fait une news huge !
example_2: T'as vu la vidéo qui circule ? C'est insane !
example_3: T'as vu le match hier ? Quel but incredible !
example_1_translation: Did you see Julie's last post? She made huge news!
example_2_translation: Have you seen the video circulating? It's insane!
example_3_translation: Did you see the match yesterday? What an incredible goal!

meaning: that's so lame / what bad luck
primary_function: Slang expression of bad luck or disappointment
communicative_purpose: To express sympathy for bad luck or describe unfortunate situations
trigger_situations: Reacting to unfortunate events, failures, unlucky circumstances
contexts: Sports disappointments, failed plans, unfortunate events, pity
variations: c'est la loose, quelle loose, c'est vraiment la loose, la loose totale
common_mistakes: Spelling variations 'lousse' or 'lousse'; taking it too seriously
collocates: triste, dommage, malchance, pas de bol
example_1: L'équipe a perdu sur le dernier but, c'est la loose.
example_2: Le bus est passé juste avant, quelle loose !
example_3: J'ai raté mon train, c'est la loose totale.
example_1_translation: The team lost on the last goal, it's lost.
example_2_translation: The bus passed just before, what a loser!
example_3_translation: I missed my train, it's a total loss.
